metadata.summary: Framework for building high-performing teams using Tuckman's stages with team charter, skills matrix, and retrospective templates. Adapts PM style per stage using tracked metrics.

content.title: Team Development
content.summary: A framework for building high-performing project teams using Tuckman's stages (Forming → Storming → Norming → Performing → Adjourning) as the diagnostic backbone, paired with three artifact types: team charter, skills matrix, and retrospective template. The PM adapts leadership style per stage and uses tracked metrics (PR attribution, contribution distribution) to move the team through stages deliberately.

why.p: Talented individuals do not automatically become a high-performing team. Without structured facilitation through Storming — the most common stuck point — conflict freezes velocity for months. Tuckman's model gives a shared language for diagnosing team health and a stage-specific action menu, reducing guesswork in leadership decisions.

list.item: Onboarding a new hire or contractor and wanting to map them onto Tuckman stages.
Sprint retro reveals interpersonal conflict (Storming) and PM needs structured mediation steps.
Solo founder hiring first 1–3 people and needing a written charter, skills matrix, and integration plan.
Tracking a team's progression so leadership knows when to push autonomy vs. provide direction.
One-off async working groups disbanding in a week — Tuckman overhead exceeds value.
Pure individual-contributor work with no inter-dependency.
Highly transactional vendor relationships — Tuckman assumes a sustained team.

rule.statement: Re-run the Tuckman stage assessment after every team-composition change (hire, departure, reorg) — not on a calendar schedule.
rule.rationale: Teams regress to Forming or Storming when key members change; calendar-based assessment misses the regression window where intervention has the highest return.

section.name: forming
section.p: Polite, cautious, high dependency on the PM for direction. Members focus on defining their role rather than executing work.

list.item: PM actions: provide explicit direction, clarify roles and responsibilities, set working agreements in writing
Evidence signals: members ask permission for small decisions, few spontaneous collaborations, low conflict but also low velocity

section.name: storming
section.p: Conflict, competition, and frustration surface. This is the highest-risk stage — teams can stay here for months without structured facilitation.

list.item: PM actions: facilitate structured discussion, mediate (meet separately first), focus conversation on shared goals not positions, document agreed decisions
Evidence signals: heated retros, PR review comments with sharp tone, recurring same argument, velocity drop, attendance issues

rule.statement: Document Storming-stage decisions in writing immediately; unrecorded verbal resolutions get re-litigated within two sprints.
rule.rationale: Heated decisions feel resolved in the moment but lack shared memory; written record prevents the "we never agreed to that" pattern.

section.name: norming
section.p: Trust building, cooperation, shared processes forming. Team starts solving problems without PM involvement.

list.item: PM actions: delegate, support, encourage cross-team ownership, step back from day-to-day mediation
Evidence signals: team resolves issues in standup without escalation, PR reviews are constructive, velocity stabilizes

section.name: performing
section.p: High productivity, autonomy, self-organizing. PM focuses on removing systemic obstacles, not directing work.

list.item: PM actions: empower decisions, remove organizational blockers, protect from external interruptions, provide strategic context
Evidence signals: team ships without PM in daily loop, retros produce non-trivial improvements, velocity at or above target

section.name: adjourning
section.p: Project closure or team restructuring. Focus on knowledge capture and individual transition.

list.item: PM actions: celebrate accomplishments, facilitate knowledge transfer, document lessons learned, support individual transitions
Evidence signals: project marked complete or team restructuring announced
Regular 1-on-1s with explicit agenda (block 30 min weekly per direct report)
Psychological safety — teams must be able to raise concerns without punishment
Transparent communication — share context behind decisions, not just decisions
Team-building activities tailored to team size and remote/in-person split

rule.statement: Keep the team charter short (one screen) and treat it as a living document; review every 8-10 weeks or after any team-composition change.
rule.rationale: Long charters are not read; short ones are consulted at conflicts. Staleness destroys trust in the document and by extension in the agreements it captures.

list.item: Skills matrix: rate each skill 1 (beginner) to 4 (expert); pair every "1" cell with a named owner and a quarterly improvement target
Pair programming and cross-training for knowledge transfer across sole-owner skills
Mentoring programs — internal, with explicit goals and check-in cadence
Training opportunities — budget per-person per quarter, not per-year

rule.statement: Skills-matrix scores must be evidence-backed (PRs, incidents, code reviews); self-reported scores without evidence drift toward inflation.
rule.rationale: Inflated matrices mask critical knowledge gaps that surface at the worst moment — when the sole expert is unavailable during an incident.

list.item: Retrospectives: every sprint; action items must have owner + due date + verification method in next retro
Process experiments: time-boxed (one sprint), measured, documented as ADR if adopted
Tool optimization: quarterly review; only add tooling that removes friction, not adds ceremony
Workload balancing: use contribution data (git shortlog, PR review counts) to surface imbalances

rule.statement: Retro action items without an owner and verification method are not action items — they are wishes.
rule.rationale: Unowned actions do not get done; unverified actions get closed as done regardless of outcome; both undermine retro trust over time.

antipattern.description: Using Tuckman stage labels as an excuse for inaction ("we're just Storming, it will pass").
antipattern.reason: Storming does not self-resolve without facilitation; teams that skip active mediation can stay in Storming for months and lose key contributors.

antipattern.description: Applying Tuckman to distributed async-only teams without adjustment.
antipattern.reason: The model assumes synchronous dynamics; async teams show attenuated stage signals and require additional indicators (response lag, async thread sentiment).

antipattern.description: Skipping team building because "there's no time."
antipattern.reason: Dysfunction deferred costs more time than investment avoided; unresolved Storming can freeze a team for months.

antipattern.description: One-size-fits-all management style across all team members.
antipattern.reason: Different Tuckman stages and different experience levels require different leadership approaches; uniform style over-manages high performers and under-supports strugglers.
Individual blame destroys psychological safety and repeats the failure; root cause is almost always process, tooling, or system design.

example.title: Navigating Storming Stage
example.note: Situation: two developers in architectural disagreement that is slowing the team and heating meetings.
example.code:
Symptoms observed: - Developer A and Developer B argue about microservice vs. monolith in every planning meeting - Retrospective atmosphere is tense; others are silent - Sprint velocity dropped 30% over 3 sprints - Both devs are updating architecture docs independently
PM actions (in order): 1. Meet individually with A and B — understand each person's technical concern and personal stake 2. Facilitate a structured session with explicit ground rules: one speaker, no interruptions, timer 3. Frame the discussion as "what does the system need?" not "who is right?" 4. Document the agreed-upon architectural decision as an ADR within 24 hours 5. Share ADR with full team; name the decision-maker if no consensus
Week 3 follow-up: - Both devs cite ADR in code reviews instead of re-litigating - Retro tone returns to constructive - Velocity recovers to baseline

example.title: Solo-to-Team Transition
example.note: Situation: solo founder hiring first team member; no existing process documentation.
example.code:
Before hire arrives: - Write down every implicit process (deployment, code review, naming conventions) - Create a "how we work" document (2 pages max) - Set up repo access, Slack, 1password, and dev environment guide
Week 1: Orientation - Walk through codebase together; explain architectural decisions - Shadow founder for 2 days: observe how decisions get made - Define initial responsibilities in writing
Weeks 2-4: Gradual Ownership - Pair on first 3 features; founder drives, new hire navigates - Swap: new hire drives, founder reviews + explains - Give feedback within 24 hours of PR submission - Weekly 1-on-1: what's unclear? what would help?
Month 2: Independence - Assign a feature with clear acceptance criteria; no pairing - Check in at midpoint, not daily - Ask new hire to document one process they think is unclear - Celebrate first solo ship in team channel
Key: document everything before hire arrives; verbal-only onboarding creates dependency on the founder and slows the transition to independence.

example.title: Skills Matrix Update from Git Data
example.note: Use git history to provide evidence-backed initial scores for a skills matrix.
example.code: # Infer expertise per directory from git contribution history (last 180 days) since="180 days ago" for dir in $(git ls-tree -d --name-only HEAD); do echo "## $dir" git log --since="$since" --pretty=format:'%an' -- "$dir" \ | sort | uniq -c | sort -rn | head -5 echo done
example.note: Use output as starting evidence; do not auto-populate scores without human review.

section.name: agentic-workflow
section.p: Use Claude subagents to keep the artifacts (charter, skills matrix, retro doc) under version control and refreshed each sprint. The PM agent generates draft deltas; the human PM/EM owns merging. Treat each Tuckman transition as a trigger event that re-runs the matrix and charter check.

list.item: faion-pm-agent — drafts charter, retro template, action items from sprint logs
faion-sdd-executor-agent — turns retro action items into SDD tasks under .aidocs/
General-purpose Claude subagent — synthesises 1-on-1 notes into a Tuckman-stage diagnosis

section.code: You are reading 6 weeks of standup notes + 2 retros for team X. Classify the team's current Tuckman stage with 3 specific quotes as evidence. Output 5 PM actions targeted to that stage only.

Update the skills matrix in <path>. New input: <PR review patterns, who reviewed what, who got stuck>. Bump scores only with at least one cited PR or incident. Flag gaps where one person is sole owner.

antipattern.description: LLMs over-pattern-match Tuckman labels without evidence.
antipattern.reason: Require evidence quotes in the diagnosis prompt to avoid false classifications.

antipattern.description: Letting an agent auto-publish a retro without human review.
antipattern.reason: Humans must redact attribution and tone before sharing.

antipattern.description: Agents writing 1-on-1 notes verbatim into shared docs.
antipattern.reason: Privacy boundary violation; separate personal feedback from team-level insights.

antipattern.description: Agents generating a generic charter instead of citing team incidents.
antipattern.reason: Force them to cite the team's actual incidents and patterns to ground the charter in reality.

antipattern.description: Skills-matrix scoring by an agent without evidence backing.
antipattern.reason: Must be tied to PRs, incidents, or code reviews; hallucinated scores hide critical gaps.

section.name: cli-tools
section.p: Tools and services for team-development workflows:

list.item: gh — Pull PR review counts to inform skills matrix (brew install gh)
git shortlog -sne --since='90 days' — Contribution distribution per teammate (git built-in)
tokei / scc — Code ownership by directory, proxy for expertise (cargo install tokei)
git-fame — Per-author line/commit attribution (gem install git_fame)
Linear — SaaS, agent-friendly REST/GraphQL, cycle reviews map to Tuckman cadence
Jira — SaaS, agent-friendly REST API, sprint retros + team velocity in one place
Pulse / Officevibe — SaaS, limited agent support, pulse surveys feed psychological-safety metric
15Five — SaaS, limited agent support, 1-on-1 notes input for stage diagnosis
Glean / GitDuck — SaaS, limited agent support, pair-programming evidence for skills matrix
Run a Tuckman self-assessment with the team after every team-composition change (hire, departure, reorg) — not on a calendar.
Keep the charter short (one screen). Living document. Review every 8–10 weeks.
Skills matrix: pair every "1" cell with a named owner who will move it to "2" within a quarter.
Retro action items must have an owner + due date + a way to verify in next retro. No anonymous TODOs.
Document Storming-stage decisions in writing — heated discussions get re-litigated unless captured.

template.purpose: Team charter: mission, members, working agreements, meeting cadence, success metrics
Skills matrix template with 1–4 scale and gap-action column
Retrospective template: went well / didn't go well / ideas / action items with owner+due
